nativeRange.habitat: Marine coastal waters, tide pools, intertidal and shallow subtidal zones, estuaries, and brackish lagoons. Typically found growing free-floating or loosely attached to substrate in nutrient-enriched shallow waters.

avoidWith: large herbivorous fish that will graze it heavily in display tanks

summary: Chaetomorpha, commonly called Chaeto, is a fast-growing filamentous green macroalgae widely regarded as the standard refugium alga for reef aquariums. It absorbs excess nitrate and phosphate directly as it grows, making it one of the most efficient tools for biological nutrient control.
careNotes: Chaetomorpha is a genus of roughly 50 species, and trade specimens are often sold without identification to species level — Chaetomorpha linum is the most commonly referenced species, but many shipments may contain similar relatives. It is widely regarded as the go-to refugium macroalgae for reef aquariums, and is generally considered one of the easiest macroalgae to keep. It generally does best when tumbled freely in a refugium compartment with strong, consistent water movement — this prevents dead spots and keeps the clump evenly exposed to light and nutrients. A 12–16 hour photoperiod under full-spectrum LED refugium lighting is commonly advised; bright, high-intensity light drives fast growth. Browning or sliminess typically indicates poor water quality or insufficient nutrients — Chaeto requires some residual nitrate (generally above ~5 ppm) and phosphate (above ~0.03 ppm) to thrive; it will decline in very nutrient-poor systems. Healthy growth is bright green and firm. Harvesting roughly one-third of the biomass every few weeks is commonly recommended to maintain vigorous new growth and export nutrients from the system. Species in this genus are cosmopolitan and have been documented tolerating brackish conditions in their natural range. Note: Chaetomorpha can become invasive if released into natural waterways; always desiccate, boil, or freeze discarded material before disposal.
breedingNotes: Chaetomorpha reproduces both asexually via fragmentation and zoospore production, and sexually through isogamous gametes (sex cells that are identical in size and appearance, unlike the distinct egg-and-sperm arrangement of most familiar animals). In aquarium settings, propagation is effectively always by simple fragmentation — breaking off a portion and placing it in the refugium. Zoospore release has been documented as a stress response under poor lighting, nutrient depletion, or lack of trimming, and can result in culture collapse.

primary.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
primary.notes: Primary trade source per manifest. Limited husbandry detail — confirms refugium use, tumbling, and adequate lighting as key requirements.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros
additional.notes: Secondary trade source. Confirms fast growth and nutrient-absorbing properties. Notes brackish-water tolerance. Also lists 'Seagrass Chaeto' variant (grass-like carpeting form).

additional.name: AlgaeBarn
additional.notes: Cross-reference source (AlgaeBarn sells C. linum as 'Clean Chaeto'). Confirms fast growth, scouring-pad tumble-ball form, bright full-spectrum lighting, nitrate/phosphate export. Notes copepod/amphipod refuge benefit.

additional.name: ReefCleaners — Macroalgae Care
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (72–86°F / 22–30°C), salinity (~1.024), pH (7.9–8.2). Confirms Chaeto as very fast growing and high nutrient export performer.

additional.name: Plankton Marin — Guide to Keeping Chaetomorpha
additional.notes: Cross-verified salinity 1.025–1.027, pH 7.8–8.5. Recommends 10–20 gallon refugium, 10–12 hours light/day. Confirms rapid doubling of biomass within days under good conditions.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chaetomorpha
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority cross-reference. Confirms Family Cladophoraceae, Order Cladophorales, Class Ulvophyceae. Cosmopolitan distribution in marine/brackish waters. ~50 species. Reproduces by fragmentation, zoospores, and isogamous gametes.
